label: Array Sum
explanation: Single pass through n elements. No auxiliary data structure — just one integer variable.

label: Binary Search (Iterative)
explanation: Each iteration halves the search space → O(log n) iterations. Two integer pointers are the only extra memory.

label: Brute-Force Pair Check
explanation: The inner loop runs up to n times for each of n outer iterations → n(n-1)/2 ≈ n² pairs checked. No extra space.

label: Two Sum with HashMap
explanation: Single pass; each HashMap operation is O(1) amortized. The map stores at most n entries in the worst case.

label: Fibonacci (Naive Recursive)
explanation: Each call branches into two, creating a binary call tree of depth n → roughly 2^n calls total. The call stack depth is at most n.

label: Merge Sort
explanation: The tree has log n levels; each level merges a total of n elements → O(n log n) work. The merge step requires O(n) auxiliary buffer.

label: Subsets (Backtracking)
explanation: Each element is either included or excluded → 2^n subsets generated. Recursion depth is at most n.

label: Generate All Permutations
explanation: Generates all n! orderings. The recursion depth equals n (one level per position placed).

label: Build Prefix Sum Array
explanation: Single pass to fill the array. Output array of size n+1 is the dominant space cost.

label: Sort Then Linear Scan
explanation: Java's Arrays.sort uses dual-pivot quicksort: O(n log n) time, O(log n) recursion depth. The subsequent linear scan is dominated by the sort.

label: Character Frequency Map
explanation: One pass of length n. The map holds at most n entries (or 26 if input is restricted to lowercase letters, but canonical analysis is O(n)).

label: Palindrome Check (Two Pointers)
explanation: Two pointers each move at most n/2 steps → O(n) comparisons total. No auxiliary memory beyond two integer indices.

label: BFS Level-Order Traversal
explanation: Each of the n nodes is enqueued and dequeued exactly once. The queue holds up to O(n) nodes (the last level of a complete binary tree has n/2 nodes).

label: String Concat in a Loop
explanation: Java Strings are immutable. Each `+=` copies the accumulated string → 1+2+…+n chars copied → O(n²). The final string is O(n) space. Always use StringBuilder instead.

label: StringBuilder Concatenation
explanation: StringBuilder amortizes appends — total characters written across all calls is n. The internal buffer (O(n)) is returned directly by toString().

label: 2D DP Table (LCS-style)
explanation: Fills an m×n table (assume m ≈ n). Each cell takes O(1). The table itself occupies n² space.

label: Recursive Tree Height (DFS)
explanation: Visits every node once → O(n). For a balanced tree the recursion stack depth equals the height, which is O(log n). (Worst case for a skewed tree is O(n).)

label: Top-K with Min-Heap
explanation: n offers/polls on a heap that grows up to k (and up to n when k≈n) → O(n log k). With k≈n this is O(n log n). Heap holds at most k ≈ n entries.

label: Counting Bits 0..n (DP)
explanation: Computes popcount for each 0..n in one pass using a DP recurrence. The output array has n+1 elements.

label: Gaussian Sum Formula
snippet:
// Sum of integers 1..n
return (long) n * (n + 1) / 2;
explanation: One multiplication, one addition, one division — all independent of n. Zero allocations.

label: Power-of-Two Bit Trick
explanation: Two bitwise operations regardless of n. A power of two has exactly one 1-bit; n & (n-1) clears it, leaving zero.

label: Count Set Bits (Brian Kernighan)
explanation: Iterates once per set bit. An integer n has at most ⌊log₂ n⌋ + 1 bits, so the loop runs O(log n) times. No auxiliary memory.

label: Sliding Window Sum
explanation: Two sequential passes (both O(n)). The window is maintained with constant-time addition and subtraction. No extra arrays.

label: GCD (Euclidean Algorithm)
explanation: Each iteration reduces the problem size by at least half (Fibonacci worst case) → O(log min(a,b)) iterations. Three integer variables only.

label: Selection Sort
explanation: Two nested loops — outer runs n-1 times, inner scans the unsorted suffix → n(n-1)/2 comparisons. Swap is in-place: O(1) space.

label: Union-Find (Path Compression)
explanation: With path compression (and union by rank) each operation is nearly O(1) amortized — technically O(α(n)) which is effectively O(log n) in practice. The parent array is O(n).

label: 1D DP (Climbing Stairs)
explanation: Single loop from 2 to n. The DP array of size n+1 is the dominant space cost. (Can be reduced to O(1) space with two variables.)

label: Fill n×n Identity Matrix
explanation: Every cell of an n×n matrix is visited once → n² iterations. The output matrix itself occupies n² space.

label: Fibonacci (Memoized)
explanation: Each distinct subproblem fib(0)..fib(n) is computed exactly once → n unique calls. Both the memo map and the recursion stack store up to n entries.

label: N-Queens (Backtracking)
explanation: In the worst case the algorithm explores n × (n-1) × … × 1 = n! placement paths. The recursion depth is n; the boolean arrays are fixed at O(n).
